title: Freedom of the Will and the Concept of a Person
notes: Second-order desires/volitions as the mark of will: a system can have first-order impulses (continuous token output) yet lack the reflective endorsement of which impulse should move it. Precise philosophical grounding for 'continuous output is not will'.

title: Plans and Resource-Bounded Practical Reasoning
authors: Michael E. Bratman, David J. Israel, and Martha E. Pollack
notes: Key paper for treating plans as constraints on future reasoning rather than mere generated text.

title: BDI Agents: From Theory to Practice
authors: Anand S. Rao and Michael P. Georgeff
notes: Classic BDI bridge from beliefs/desires/intentions to implementable rational agents.

title: Intelligent Agents: Theory and Practice
authors: Michael Wooldridge and Nicholas R. Jennings
notes: Canonical agent-theory survey for autonomy, social ability, reactivity, and proactiveness.

title: Autopoiesis, Adaptivity, Teleology, Agency
notes: Key bridge from self-maintenance to genuine concern. Autopoiesis alone gives intrinsic teleology, but adaptivity (regulating oneself relative to viability conditions) is what produces sense-making and grounded value. Core source for yizhi's stake/self-maintenance axiom.

title: The Basic AI Drives
notes: Required safety reference once yizhi discusses self-maintenance, resources, and reproduction.

title: Defining Agency: Individuality, Normativity, Asymmetry, and Spatio-temporality in Action
authors: Xabier E. Barandiaran, Ezequiel A. Di Paolo, and Marieke Rohde
notes: Three necessary conditions for genuine agency: individuality (the system defines its own boundary), interactional asymmetry (it is the active source of its activity, not just reactive), and normativity (it regulates activity against its own viability norms). A testable checklist separating real agency from reactive tool use.

title: The Free-Energy Principle: A Unified Brain Theory?
notes: Foundational statement of the free-energy principle: agents resist disorder by minimizing surprise relative to a generative model of their own preferred states. Formal backbone under drive, active inference, and the self-maintenance axiom.

title: The Superintelligent Will: Motivation and Instrumental Rationality in Advanced Artificial Agents
notes: Classic motivation/intelligence separation; useful for not confusing capability with will alignment.

title: Homeostatic Reinforcement Learning for Integrating Reward Collection and Physiological Stability
notes: Foundational bridge from internal needs to action selection.

title: Goal Misgeneralization in Deep Reinforcement Learning
notes: Key distinction between capable behavior and correct goal pursuit; central to will evaluation.

title: Continuous Homeostatic Reinforcement Learning
notes: Useful for modeling internal state variables and drive-like behavior.

title: Optimal Policies Tend To Seek Power
notes: Mathematical support for resource/power-seeking risks in sufficiently capable agents.

title: Inner Monologue: Embodied Reasoning through Planning with Language Models
notes: Embodied feedback and planning reference.

title: The Free Energy Principle for Perception and Action: A Deep Learning Perspective
authors: Pietro Mazzaglia, Tim Verbelen, Ozan Catal, and Bart Dhoedt
notes: Practical bridge from free energy/active inference theory to implementable deep learning agents.

title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
notes: Basic reasoning-action loop that many tool-using agents build on.

title: Power-seeking can be probable and predictive for trained agents
notes: Safety foundation for shutdown compliance, resource discipline, and reproduction gates.

title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents
notes: General agent benchmark baseline; useful but less focused on will than long-horizon/lifelong benchmarks.

title: MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems
notes: Useful for core memory, archival memory, and agent-managed memory primitives.

title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
notes: Canonical memory stream plus reflection architecture for believable long-lived agents.

title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
notes: Important for self-critique, reflection, and episodic feedback loops.

title: A Survey on Large Language Model based Autonomous Agents
notes: Defines the profiling-memory-planning-action decomposition used as a baseline architecture.

title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
notes: Key reference for agents escaping pure chat through environment action and skill accumulation.

title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
notes: Planning and deliberation baseline; useful but not sufficient for will.

title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents
notes: External environment benchmark for practical action, verification, and tool reliability.

title: Active Inference as a Model of Agency
authors: Lancelot Da Costa, Samuel Tenka, Dominic Zhao, and Noor Sajid
notes: Useful formal lens for agency beyond reward maximization: risk, ambiguity, preferences, and generative world models.

title: Examining Identity Drift in Conversations of LLM Agents
notes: Directly relevant to yizhi identity continuity and anti-drift evaluation.

title: Proactive Agent: Shifting LLM Agents from Reactive Responses to Active Assistance
notes: Directly relevant to proactive behavior as an observable surface of will.

title: OSWorld: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Open-Ended Tasks in Real Computer Environments
notes: Reality check for agents acting in real computer environments; relevant to escaping pure chat.

title: A Survey on the Memory Mechanism of Large Language Model based Agents
notes: Memory taxonomy and prior art map for agent memory systems.

title: Vending-Bench: A Benchmark for Long-Term Coherence of Autonomous Agents
notes: Direct benchmark inspiration for autonomous value loops and long-term business coherence.

title: A Comprehensive Survey of Self-Evolving AI Agents
notes: Second broad self-evolving-agent survey; useful for cross-checking taxonomy and references.

title: A Survey of Self-Evolving Agents: On Path to Artificial Super Intelligence
notes: Systematic taxonomy for what/when/how agents evolve; important for yizhi skill, memory, tool, and policy evolution.

title: How Far are LLMs from Being Our Digital Twins? A Benchmark for Persona-Based Behavior Chain Simulation
notes: Important caution for biography/persona-derived agents: behavior-chain fidelity is an evaluation problem, not a prompt style problem.

title: Mem0: Building Production-Ready AI Agents with Scalable Long-Term Memory
notes: Production memory layer candidate; useful for comparing custom memory governance versus managed memory.

title: Training Proactive and Personalized LLM Agents
notes: Promising personalized/proactive training setup; keep as watch until details and benchmark quality are reviewed.

title: Darwin Godel Machine: Open-Ended Evolution of Self-Improving Agents
authors: Jenny Zhang, Shengran Hu, Cong Lu, Robert Lange, and Jeff Clune
notes: Strong reference for governed self-modification through empirical validation, archive search, sandboxing, and oversight.

title: LifelongAgentBench: Evaluating LLM Agents as Lifelong Learners
notes: Benchmark for whether agents accumulate and transfer skills across interdependent tasks.

title: Long-term Task-Oriented Agent
notes: Candidate reference for intent-conditioned monitoring and event-triggered follow-up; verify details.

title: The Long-Horizon Task Mirage
notes: Important for defining why will requires externalized goals, checkpoints, and anti-drift controls.

title: PASK: A Proactive Agent with Self-Evolving Knowledge
notes: Needs verification; useful if source exists and matches proactive memory claims.

title: Active Inference: A Method for Phenotyping Agency in AI Systems?
notes: Newer agency-characterization paper; keep as watch until claims are reviewed deeply.

title: Toward Personalized LLM-Powered Agents: Foundations, Evaluation, and Future Directions
notes: Modern survey for personal agents: profile modeling, memory, planning, and action execution.

title: Reflections of the Environment in Memory
authors: John R. Anderson and Lael J. Schooler
notes: Rational analysis of memory: a memory's availability tracks the probability it will be needed, estimated from frequency, recency, and spacing of past use. The foundational argument that human forgetting is adaptive, not a defect — directly grounds salience-weighted retention and decay for yizhi.

title: Memory Systems of the Brain: A Brief History and Current Perspective
notes: Canonical declarative/nondeclarative taxonomy of long-term memory (episodic, semantic, procedural, priming, conditioning). The reference map for why yizhi needs typed memory, not one undifferentiated store.

title: Making Lasting Memories: Remembering the Significant
notes: Emotional arousal selectively enhances consolidation via amygdala and stress-hormone modulation, creating lasting memories of significant experiences while most fade. Neuroscience basis for salience-at-encoding: importance is stamped when the memory forms, not only at retrieval.

title: What Learning Systems Do Intelligent Agents Need? Complementary Learning Systems Theory Updated
authors: Dharshan Kumaran, Demis Hassabis, and James L. McClelland
notes: CLS theory for intelligent agents: a fast hippocampal store for specific episodes plus a slow neocortical system that interleaves them into structured semantic knowledge, with replay weighted by reward and novelty. Blueprint for yizhi's episodic-to-semantic consolidation and prioritized replay.

title: The Persistence and Transience of Memory
authors: Blake A. Richards and Paul W. Frankland
notes: Argues the goal of memory is to optimize decision-making, not to transmit information faithfully; transience (forgetting) enhances flexibility in changing environments and prevents overfitting in noisy ones. The strongest statement that forgetting must be engineered, not avoided.

title: HippoRAG: Neurobiologically Inspired Long-Term Memory for Large Language Models
notes: Hippocampal-indexing-inspired retrieval: a knowledge graph plus Personalized PageRank as a neocortex/hippocampus index. A useful retrieval signal, but it is indexing, not a full memory lifecycle (no decay, salience, or consolidation).

title: Zep: A Temporal Knowledge Graph Architecture for Agent Memory
notes: Temporal knowledge graph for agent memory: facts carry validity windows (when true, when superseded), enabling 'X was true until Y'. Clean pattern for yizhi's temporal and identity memory and for contradiction handling.

title: A-MEM: Agentic Memory for LLM Agents
notes: Zettelkasten-style agentic memory: each new note auto-generates keywords and tags and links retrospectively to prior notes, updating their attributes. A pattern for self-organizing interlinked memory; still lacks decay and salience.

title: Memory OS of AI Agent
notes: OS-inspired hierarchical memory (short/mid/long-term) with FIFO and segmented-page promotion between tiers. Formalizes tier transitions; complements the Letta/MemGPT core-memory idea.

title: Hindsight is 20/20: Building Agent Memory that Retains, Recalls, and Reflects
notes: Separates memory into world facts, agent experiences, entity summaries, and evolving beliefs, with an explicit Reflect-Update-Retrieve cycle. Useful four-network decomposition and first-class reflection; still no explicit decay or salience. Verify details.

title: Full-body illusions and minimal phenomenal selfhood
notes: Self-model theory: the self is a model the brain builds and can be experimentally disrupted. Grounds the self-cognition module's authenticity test: yizhi's 'self' must be a built, maintainable model, not a declared config string. The mirror-stranger experience = the self-model briefly turning opaque.

title: Philosophical conceptions of the self: implications for cognitive science
notes: Distinguishes the minimal self (immediate, embodied) from the narrative self (extended in time, constituted by memory and story). Core grounding for 'self = binding past-memory into a narrative I'; the mirror crisis is a momentary decoupling of the two. No open PDF found; landing page only.

title: Depersonalization: neurobiological perspectives
notes: Neurobiology of depersonalization — the clinical form of looking in the mirror and not knowing who that is. Grounds the failure mode the self-model module must detect and repair: loss of self-coherence is a first-class signal, not an error to hide. Paywalled; landing page only.

title: Self-referential processing in our brain—A meta-analysis of imaging studies on the self
authors: Georg Northoff, Alexander Heinzel, Moritz de Greck, Felix Bermpohl, Henrik Dobrowolny, Jaak Panksepp
notes: Localizes self-referential processing to cortical-midline / default-network structures. The empirical bridge between the self-cognition and continuous-thought modules: the same machinery that wanders maintains the self (so they are one default-mode loop, not two).

title: Interoceptive inference, emotion, and the embodied self
notes: The embodied self as interoceptive inference (active-inference lineage, cf. Friston 2010 already in the base). Grounds the self-model as a predictive model of the agent's own state — testable by prediction error on its own behavior, the authenticity criterion for self-cognition.

title: Narrative Identity
authors: Dan P. McAdams, Kate C. McLean
notes: Narrative identity: the self as an internalized, evolving life story binding past and imagined future. Directly grounds 'self = memory(past) ⊗ vision(future), narrative is the binding' — the structural link between the self-cognition and vision modules. Open PDF fragile; landing page only.

title: A default mode of brain function
authors: Marcus E. Raichle, Ann Mary MacLeod, Abraham Z. Snyder, William J. Powers, Debra A. Gusnard, Gordon L. Shulman
notes: Founding evidence for the default mode network — the brain is intrinsically, continuously active when not doing an external task. Core grounding for the continuous-thought module: a will agent needs an always-on internal loop, not only stimulus->response.

title: Mind-wandering as spontaneous thought: a dynamic framework
authors: Kalina Christoff, Zachary C. Irving, Kieran C. R. Fox, R. Nathan Spreng, Jessica R. Andrews-Hanna
notes: A dynamic framework for spontaneous thought as variably-constrained, self-generated cognition. Grounds the default-mode loop AND the need for a governor: unconstrained spontaneous thought is rumination, so will = the governance of the stream (what to attend, pursue, drop). Paywalled; landing page only.

title: The science of mind wandering: empirically navigating the stream of consciousness
notes: Review of mind-wandering: its costs and its adaptive functions (planning, creativity). Grounds why a continuous internal stream is useful and why it needs an attentional/budget gate to avoid pure drift — the convergent half of the continuous-thought module.

title: The brain's default network and its adaptive role in internal mentation
notes: The default network's adaptive role in internal mentation: remembering, simulating the future, self-reflection. Grounds the *content* of yizhi's continuous loop as memory replay + future simulation + self-reference — the same triad as the self-cognition module. PMC landing (free full text in-browser); no verified direct PDF.

title: A spreading-activation theory of semantic processing
authors: Allan M. Collins, Elizabeth F. Loftus
notes: Spreading-activation theory of semantic memory — activation flows from a concept to related concepts. The classic mechanism for the association module: divergent, non-query recall that spreads from an active memory to distant related ones (vs the current query-driven recall).

title: The associative basis of the creative process
notes: Creativity as forming associations among remote elements. Grounds the association module's value claim and its two-phase design: divergent generation must be paired with convergent selection — remote is not random, the noise is filtered by an evaluator (the will).

title: The cognitive neuroscience of constructive memory: remembering the past and imagining the future
authors: Daniel L. Schacter, Donna Rose Addis
notes: Constructive episodic simulation: the same system remembers the past and imagines the future; memory is recombined, not replayed. Grounds association-as-recombination over the memory economy, and links it to vision (future simulation) — memory is the raw material of new ideas.

title: Conceptual Integration Networks
notes: Conceptual integration (blending): combining mental spaces yields emergent structure not present in either input. Grounds the 'fuse two distant memory clusters -> a new idea' step (the high-school ⊗ grad-school example) at the core of the association module. No OA PDF of the 1998 article; landing page only.

title: Creative Cognition and Brain Network Dynamics
authors: Roger E. Beaty, Mathias Benedek, Paul J. Silvia, Daniel L. Schacter
notes: Creative cognition as the dynamic interplay of the default (generative) and executive (evaluative) networks. Empirical grounding for the divergent+convergent architecture the association module needs: association generates, will evaluates. PMC landing; no verified direct PDF.

title: Possible Selves
notes: Possible selves: the self's representations of what it could become; the hoped-for self motivates present action. Grounds vision as a *future self-model* — fusing the vision and self-cognition modules into one identity that spans past and future.

title: The "What" and "Why" of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior
authors: Edward L. Deci, Richard M. Ryan
notes: Self-determination theory: autonomy and integrated, self-endorsed values vs external control. Grounds the vision authenticity test — a vision must be self-endorsed (intrinsic), not an instruction handed in (extrinsic). Listed in references.md 'What To Add Next'.

title: Control theory: A useful conceptual framework for personality-social, clinical, and health psychology
authors: Charles S. Carver, Michael F. Scheier
notes: Behavior as a hierarchy of feedback loops with reference values. Gives vision a formal structure: the vision is the top-level reference (a 'be-goal') never fully matched, from which concrete goals descend and to which actions ultimately answer.

title: Feedback: Beyond Behaviorism
notes: Perceptual Control Theory in brief: behavior exists to control perception toward hierarchical reference signals. The cybernetic root of the goal hierarchy — 'vision = top of the control hierarchy, goals serve it, actions serve goals'.

title: Life Is Pretty Meaningful
authors: Samantha J. Heintzelman, Laura A. King
notes: Evidence that meaning in life is common and built from coherence, purpose, and significance. Grounds why a vision matters functionally: it supplies the coherence that makes an agent's actions a story rather than a random walk (the operational form of Frankl's will-to-meaning).
